claim_status: No-claim web-platform standards, interoperability, and conformance-source observations only; these sources do not establish Turing compatibility, standards conformance, security, accessibility, performance, or production readiness.

sources.title: HTML Living Standard
sources.observation: The HTML standard is a living normative source whose algorithms, parsing behavior, document lifecycle, and integration points must be identified by exact retrieval or pinned snapshot when used as evidence.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record the exact source revision or retrieval identity, covered algorithms, implementation feature mapping, test mapping, unsupported behavior, and any local interpretation.

sources.title: DOM Standard
sources.observation: DOM behavior spans trees, events, mutation, ranges, selection, realms, and integration hooks; a DOM implementation cannot be represented by page screenshots alone.
sources.evidence_consequence: Retain algorithm/spec identity, semantic trace cases, realm and lifecycle conditions, event ordering, mutation outcomes, test denominator, failures, and unsupported rows.

sources.title: Fetch Standard
sources.observation: Fetch integrates request policy, origins, credentials, redirects, response bodies, streams, service workers, and network errors; a successful HTTP request is not a Fetch conformance result.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record origin/policy identity, credentials mode, redirects, timing, cancellation, body/error behavior, network fixtures, security review, and failure denominator.

sources.title: CSS Working Group drafts
sources.observation: CSS is a family of specifications with distinct maturity and test coverage; an implementation must identify the exact module, feature, syntax, computed-value behavior, layout/paint effect, and status.
sources.evidence_consequence: Pin the module and revision, map parser/style/layout/paint behavior to tests, preserve interop differences, and list unsupported or experimental features explicitly.

sources.title: ECMAScript Language Specification
sources.observation: ECMAScript language and built-in semantics are normative inputs distinct from host APIs, browser integration, and implementation performance.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record specification edition or revision, Test262 commit and harness configuration, feature categories, implementation-defined behavior, realm/agent conditions, failures, and unsupported cases.

sources.title: Test262
sources.observation: Test262 is a conformance test suite whose result depends on the exact repository commit, harness, features, host configuration, and included or excluded tests.
sources.evidence_consequence: Retain commit, harness, feature metadata, test selection, pass/fail/timeout/crash/not-run counts, expected failures, exclusions, and reproducible artifacts.

sources.title: Web Platform Tests
sources.observation: WPT provides cross-implementation web-platform tests, but results require a pinned tree, metadata, harness, environment, test selection, and complete denominator accounting.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record exact WPT commit, local patches, manifest generation, enabled directories, pass/fail/timeout/crash/leak/not-run/harness-error counts, expected failures, platform differences, and rerun policy.

sources.title: Interop project
sources.observation: Interop provides a shared prioritization and measurement context, not a complete browser-support contract or a substitute for Turing-owned test and failure accounting.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record the Interop year/area, participating-engine context, selected tests, denominator, known differences, local results, and reasons for any divergence or exclusion.

sources.title: WebDriver BiDi
sources.observation: WebDriver BiDi is a protocol surface with navigation, browsing-context, input, script, and event semantics that must be versioned and tested independently from a browser UI or agent authority model.
sources.evidence_consequence: Record protocol revision, command/event coverage, session and browsing-context identity, authorization boundary, transport faults, conformance tests, and unsupported commands.

sources.title: Web Platform Design Principles
sources.observation: Web-platform design principles require compatibility, user needs, accessibility, privacy, security, and interoperability considerations when proposing new platform behavior.
sources.evidence_consequence: Each proposed or experimental feature needs a user need, standards/interoperability route, privacy/security/accessibility review, test plan, multi-implementer rationale, and explicit deprecation or rejection path.

unsupported: No HTML, DOM, CSS, Fetch, ECMAScript, WPT, Test262, Interop, or WebDriver BiDi execution has been established by this manifest.
No exact standards snapshot, WPT/Test262 denominator, browser-run artifact package, or differential result is accepted as current compatibility evidence here.
No feature is selected for implementation, no web-platform API is supported, and no compatibility exception or user-agent intervention is approved.
No standards conformance, Chrome-class compatibility, security, accessibility, performance, production, or implementation claim exists.
